coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated zinc cobaltite nanowires-assembled hierarchical arrays supported on nickel foam as binder-free electrodes for high performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Caixia
Zhao, Zhe
Liu, Yang
Lu, Qingshan
coredata.dc:description: Hierarchical and binder-free electrode materials with large electroactive sites and low contact resistance are benefit for electrochemical capacitors. Herein, this paper reports the binder-free and hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays decorated with carbon dots (CDs/ZnCo2O4) on nickel foam by hydrothermal route. The CDs/ZnCo2O4 arrays exhibit the nanowires-assembled hierarchical grass-like structure. The outward directed nanowires yield high surface-to-volume ratio. The nanowires exhibit the average length of 1 μm and diameter of 50 nm. The specific capacitance of the CDs/ZnCo2O4 can reach 1937 F/g higher than the 1192 F/g of bare ZnCo2O4 at 1 A/g. And 78.5% capacitance retention rate is achieved while the current density increases from 0.5 to 8 A/g. 90% of the capacitance retention over 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 15 A/g indicates high cycling stability. These boosted electrochemical performances are attributed to the structural feature with exposed electroactive sites, high electrical conductivity assisted by CDs, and opened interspaces for electrolyte ion diffusion. Asymmetric two-electrode system assembled with CDs/ZnCo2O4 and activated carbon delivers a high-energy density of 54.94 W h/kg at 425.3 W/kg and retains an energy density of 13.23 W h/kg at 6804 W/kg. This study provides a new approach to design high-performance electrode materials for supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc cobaltite
Hierarchical structure
Carbon dots
Supercapacitors

Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under 5196202 6 and Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation under 2018MS05014 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation process of CDs/ZnCo2O4.
(a–g) SEM images of CDs/ZnCo2O4 with different resolutions and (h) XRD patterns of ZnCo2O4 and CDs/ZnCo2O4 on Ni foam.
(a–f) TEM images, (g) high-resolution image, (h) selected area electron diffraction pattern, and (i–m) element mapping of CDs/ZnCo2O4.
(a) XPS full spectrum and corresponding high–resolution spectrum of (b) Zn 2p, (c) Co 2p, (d) O 1s, and (e) C 1s of CDs/ZnCo2O4.
CV curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4 with various CDs loadings at different scan rates (a–c) and at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 (d).

(a–c) GCD curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4 with various CDs loadings at different current densities, (d) correlation between the specific capacitance and the current density for CDs/ZnCo2O4, (e) Nyquist plots of CDs/ZnCo2O4, and (f) cycling performance together with Coulombic efficiency of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 at 15A/g.

(a) CV curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 and AC at 20 mV s−1, (b) CV curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC at various scan rates, (c) GCD curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC at various current densities, (d) dependence of calculated specific capacitance with current density for CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC, (e) Ragone plots of various ZnCo2O4-based supercapacitors, and (f) cycling performance together with coulombic efficiency of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC two-electrode system lighting up a red-light emitting diode.
Comparison of the specific capacitances of various ZnCo2O4-based electrodes.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated zinc cobaltite nanowires-assembled hierarchical arrays supported on nickel foam as binder-free electrodes for high performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology and Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hierarchical and binder-free electrode materials with large electroactive sites and low contact resistance are benefit for electrochemical capacitors. Herein, this paper reports the binder-free and hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays decorated with carbon dots (CDs/ZnCo2O4) on nickel foam by hydrothermal route. The CDs/ZnCo2O4 arrays exhibit the nanowires-assembled hierarchical grass-like structure. The outward directed nanowires yield high surface-to-volume ratio. The nanowires exhibit the average length of 1 μm and diameter of 50 nm. The specific capacitance of the CDs/ZnCo2O4 can reach 1937 F/g higher than the 1192 F/g of bare ZnCo2O4 at 1 A/g. And 78.5% capacitance retention rate is achieved while the current density increases from 0.5 to 8 A/g. 90% of the capacitance retention over 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 15 A/g indicates high cycling stability. These boosted electrochemical performances are attributed to the structural feature with exposed electroactive sites, high electrical conductivity assisted by CDs, and opened interspaces for electrolyte ion diffusion. Asymmetric two-electrode system assembled with CDs/ZnCo2O4 and activated carbon delivers a high-energy density of 54.94 W h/kg at 425.3 W/kg and retains an energy density of 13.23 W h/kg at 6804 W/kg. This study provides a new approach to design high-performance electrode materials for supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors as energy storage devices store the electric energy through the surface-depended faradaic reactions or electrical double layer attraction [1]. The surface and interface of the electrode materials dominate the electrochemical performances such as specific capacitance, rate capability, and energy density [2–4]. Compared with common nanomaterials, hierarchical structures assembled with nano-sized building blocks exhibit potential applications in electrochemical capacitors owing to their porous architecture, large surface areas, and abundant electroactive sites [5,6]. Such characteristics not only enhances the easy accessibility of electrolyte ions, but also achieves the sufficient utilization of electroactive sites for redox reactions. Especially, the binder-free electrodes can low the contact resistance among various components and reduce the inactive surface of electroactive materials [6,7]. This strategy can accelerate the electron transfer, yielding remarkable electrochemical performance. Furthermore, the direct growth of electroactive materials on current collector can avoid the complex preparation procedures of electrodes.

Zinc cobaltite (ZnCo2O4) possesses a spinel structure, in which Zn2+ and Co3+ occupy the tetrahedral and the octahedral sites, respectively [8–10]. The ZnCo2O4 is regarded as the promising candidates of pseudocapacitive materials in term of the multifarious chemical components and easy electron transfer among multiple cations in contrast to single metal oxides. Particularly, the Co3+ contributes on the capacity, while the Zn2+ provides the electron transport pathway [11]. Such feature yields a synergistic effect on the faradaic reactions. Until now, various hierarchical ZnCo2O4 have been directly grown on conductive substrates and these electrodes deliver high specific capacitance and long-term cycling stability [12]. Cheng et al. reported mesoporous ZnCo2O4 nanoflakes grown on nickel foam, which delivered an excellent specific capacitance of 1220 F/g at 2 A g−1 and a long-term cyclic stability after 5000 cycles [13]. Wang et al. designed self-supporting ZnCo2O4 nanowire arrays applied as pseudocapacitive electrodes with high specific capacitance of 1625 F/g at 5 A g−1 and excellent rate capability of 59% capacitance retention at 80 A g−1 [14]. Jadhav et al. demonstrated the binder-free and hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays, exhibiting superior specific capacitance of 1143 F/g at 1.25 A g−1 and long-term cycling stability [15]. However, the hierarchical ZnCo2O4 encounters the poor redox kinetics and the obtained capacitance is still below its theoretical value due to poor intrinsic electrical conductivity and low ion diffusion rate. Therefore, the reasonable design of novel ZnCo2O4-based electrodes is an effective strategy to address these issues. To date, considerable studies have aimed to adopt the composite strategy to boost the overall electrochemical performance of hierarchical ZnCo2O4 [9,16–19]. Quantum dot surface engineering towards ZnCo2O4 is highly desired for the high-performance supercapacitors. Particularly, the simple and effective fabrication method is highly required in view of the good scalability for practical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) with the size below 10 nm are promising conductive dopants for electrode materials [20–22]. CDs with high specific surface areas can carry enough hydrophilic groups for surface wettability. Recently, CDs have been introduced into various pseudocapacitive electrodes to improve the electrochemical performances. Hydrophilic CDs were decorated on NiCo2O4 nanowires to increase the electrical conductivity and improve the wettability of electrodes [23,24]. CDs decorated hierarchical nickel hydroxide exhibited high specific capacity, excellent rate performance, and remarkable cycling stability [25,26]. It is noticed that the carbon inhibits the reactions between metal oxides and electrolytes. In particular, excess carbon component results in the decrease of the specific capacitance, because carbon possesses relatively low capacity due to the electrical double layer behavior [27]. Especially, the particle size, morphology, and size distribution of CDs in the electrode materials greatly influence the electrochemical performances. In addition, the lower production cost of CDs expands their practical application in comparison with carbon nanotube and graphene. Considering these advantages of self-supporting hierarchical ZnCo2O4 and CDs, the rational decoration of CDs towards binder-free ZnCo2O4 probably gives rise to the remarkable electrochemical performances, which is expected to create the excellent electrode materials for supercapacitors.

In this work, hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays decorated with CDs were synthesized on nickel foam by a facile hydrothermal route (CDs/ZnCo2O4). The CDs/ZnCo2O4 arrays exhibited hierarchical grass-like structure assembled with nanowires, providing enough electroactive sites and shortened pathway for mass transfer. The specific capacitance of CDs/ZnCo2O4 was increased by 62.5% after introducing CDs. Two-electrode system assembled with CDs/ZnCo2O4 and activated carbon delivered a high-energy density of 54.94 W h/kg at a power density of 425.3 W/kg and retained an energy density of 13.23 W h/kg at a high-power density of 6804 W/kg.

All chemicals involved in this work were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without any purification. Dopamine hydrochloride (C8H11NO2·HCl, 98%) and o-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. Ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.7%), urea (CO(NH2)2, 99%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38%), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 99%), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99%), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 85%), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 98–99%), activated carbon (AC, specific surface area = 2000 m2/g), and ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 96%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nickel foam was purchased from Xingshengjie Tech. Co., Ltd.

CDs were synthesized by a bottom-up chemical oxidation method [28,29], and the synthesis process was given in supplementary material. CDs/ZnCo2O4 supported on Ni foam was prepared as follows: 2 mmol Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 4 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O were added into 35 mL of deionized water under stirring for 20 min to form a pink solution. Then, 4 mmol NH4F, 10 mmol CO(NH2)2, and CDs solution were added into the above solution under stirring. This suspension and the pretreated Ni foam (1.5*1.0 cm) were put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 120 °C for 6 h. After that, the sample was cooled to room temperature naturally, washed with deionized water, dried at 60 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven, and calcined to 350 °C for 2 h at Ar atmosphere. Finally, CDs/ZnCo2O4 supported on Ni foam was obtained. The loading mass of CDs/ZnCo2O4 on Ni foam was about 5.6 mg by determining the increased weight of Ni foam after the growth of CDs/ZnCo2O4. While testing electrochemical performance, the Ni foam with CDs/ZnCo2O4 was cut into the size of 1.0*1.0 cm. Meanwhile, the mass loading of CDs/ZnCo2O4 was decreased to 3.7 mg. Therefore, the areal mass loading was 3.7 mg/cm2. The samples prepared with different CDs solution volumes of 0, 0.5, and 1 mL were labeled as CDs/ZnCo2O4-0, CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5, and CDs/ZnCo2O4-1, respectively.

Two-electrode system was fabricated employing CDs/ZnCo2O4 as positive electrode, activated carbon (AC) as negative electrode, and KOH/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as solid electrolyte. The detailed fabrication process was illustrated in supplementary material.

X–ray diffractometer (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), Raman spectra, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence spectra (PL) were involved to characterize samples. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to measure electrochemical performances. The detailed information was shown in supplementary material.

The fabrication process of the CDs/ZnCo2O4 supported on Ni foam is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, metal salts, urea, carbon dots, and solvent are mixed together. Under hydrothermal treatment, nanowires-assembled zinc-cobalt hydroxide arrays are directly grown on Ni foam. Such hierarchical structure endows enough open interspaces, large specific surface areas, and high surface energy [30]. The electrostatic forces will drive the self-assembly of hierarchical zinc-cobalt hydroxide arrays with ultrafine CDs [25]. After calcination at Ar atmosphere, zinc-cobalt hydroxide transforms into zinc cobaltite, while its morphology maintains the initial shape of zinc-cobalt hydroxide. Finally, CDs decorated ZnCo2O4 grass-like arrays supported on Ni foam are successfully prepared.

The characterization of the CDs is shown in Fig. S1. Dark spots with spherical shape are monodispersed and the particle size is nearly uniform. The size distribution is centered around 2.6 nm. Under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, an intensive peak centered around 560 nm is observed for CDs. The fluorescence digital photograph of the CDs solution displays the yellow-green color in accordance with the emission spectra. These results are consistent with literature reports [31], further confirming the successful formation of desired CDs by the bottom-up chemical oxidation method.

The morphology of CDs/ZnCo2O4 was investigated by FE-SEM. Fig. 2 (a–g) show the SEM images with different resolutions. As shown in Fig. 2(a and b), continuous layers are observed over the Ni foam skeleton, which convinces the direct growth of CDs/ZnCo2O4 on Ni foam. The CDs/ZnCo2O4 displays the hierarchical arrays illustrated in Fig. 2(c and d). The high-magnification images in Fig. 2(e and f) suggest that the CDs/ZnCo2O4 arrays exhibit the hierarchical architectures that are assembled with high-density nanowire. The nanowires grow mostly along the radial direction, giving rise to the grass-like structure with rough and porous surface. Such remarkable exposed surface areas can provide adequate electroactive sites and open interspaces among nanowires [10,32]. The tail ends of nanowires are intertwined into a bundle in order to reduce the surface energy. Such grass-like morphology is similar to that of bare ZnCoO4 on Ni foam illustrated in Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. 2g, the nanowires exhibit the average diameter of 50 nm. It is noticed that CDs are not observed in this nanowire-assembled hierarchical structure. This is probably ascribed to both the ultrafine size and low scattering contrast between carbon dots and ZnCo2O4 [33]. The crystalline phases were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2h, the characteristic diffusion peaks are indexed to crystalline ZnCo2O4 belonging to a spinel structure [10], proving the successful formation of pure ZnCo2O4 on Ni foam substrate. CDs/ZnCo2O4 displays the same diffraction pattern as that of pristine ZnCo2O4. No diffraction peaks ascribed to CDs are detected. Two possible reasons account for the absence of diffraction peaks. Firstly, the loading content of CDs in the composites is much small. Secondly, low crystallinity or non-crystalline CDs are highly dispersed in the hierarchical structure.

Microstructures of the CDs/ZnCo2O4 are investigated by TEM observation. Low-resolution TEM images in Fig. 3 (a and b) exhibit one-dimensional nanostructure in a large region. The crosslinking one-dimensional structures indicate that the hierarchical arrays are assembled with the nanowires as building blocks. The outwards directed nanowires lead to cross each other due to their random orientation, which greatly increases the surface-to-volume ratio. This is in coincidence with the feature of grass-like morphology verified by SEM. The images in Fig. 3 (c and d) illustrates that the single nanowire exhibits the length of 1 μm and the diameter of 50 nm. The magnified images in Fig. 3 (e and f) reveal that the nanowire is consisted of interconnected nanoparticles, giving rise to the porous characteristic of ZnCo2O4. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 3g shows the lattice fringes with the interplanar spacings of 0.202, 0.244, and 0.286 nm which are indexed as the (400), (311), and (220) crystal planes of ZnCo2O4, respectively [34]. The selected area electron diffraction pattern in Fig. 3h suggests the polycrystalline nature of ZnCo2O4. The element mapping is performed on an individual nanowire illustrated in Fig. 3i. Noticeably, Co, Zn, and O elements show similar spatial profile in Fig. 3(j–m) like the configuration of the selected nanowire. This is in accord with the compound nature and morphology of ZnCo2O4. The image of C elemental mapping confirms the presence of CDs in hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays (Fig. 3m). Based on the above results, it is concluded that carbon dots decorated ZnCo2O4 nanowires-assembled hierarchical arrays have been successfully prepared using this simple method. In addition, the TEM images of bare ZnCo2O4 are given in Fig. S3. The bare ZnCo2O4 displays the hierarchical arrays assembled with nanowires as building blocks. The distinct lattice fringes are also observed in HRTEM image. It is indicated that the introduction of CDs does not obviously change the microstructure of the hierarchical ZnCo2O4.

The valence states of elements were investigated by XPS analysis. Fig. 4 a shows the full spectrum of the hierarchical CDs/ZnCo2O4 arrays. The characteristic peaks corresponding to Zn 2p, Co 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are observed, which is in accordance with the chemical components. As shown in Fig. 4b, the high-resolution spectrum of Zn 2p demonstrates two peaks with the binding energies of 1044.8 and 1021.7 eV which are respectively ascribed to the Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 of the Zn2+ in ZnCo2O4 [8]. With regard to the spectrum of Co 2p in Fig. 4c, the peaks with the binding energies of 782.7 and 780.8 eV respectively correspond to the Co 2p3/2 of Co2+ and Co3+, together with the shakeup satellite peak at 786.3 eV. The peaks with the binding energy of 798.2 and 796.8 eV of Co 2p1/2 respectively belong to Co2+ and Co3+, accompanied by the shakeup satellite peak at 803.7 eV [35]. The spin-orbit splitting value between Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 is about 15.8 eV, proving the presence of both Co2+ and Co3+. For the O 1s spectrum in Fig. 4d, the peak centered at 529.6 eV are originated from O2− species in ZnCo2O4 and the peak with the binding energy of 531.4 eV is indicative of the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs. These are accordant with the reported values [10]. Fig. 4e shows the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s. The spectrum is fitted into three peaks. The peak with the binding energy of 284.8 eV can be ascribed to the C-C bond of CDs. Other two peaks at 286.2 and 287.9 eV correspond to C-O/C-N and C=O bonds of CDs, respectively, proving the presence of groups such as -NH2, -COOH, and -OH on the surface of CDs [23]. Such XPS results further indicate the successful decoration of CDs on hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays.

The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of samples are shown in Fig. S4. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms indicates that all samples possess mesoporous structure [18,27]. The specific surface areas of ZnCo2O4, CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5, and CDs/ZnCo2O4-1 are calculated to be 71.3, 65.9 and 62.4 m2/g, respectively. Based on BJH model, the pore-size distributions determined from the adsorption branches are centered from 4 to 11 nm. Such porous structure is in accordance with the TEM results. It is found that the specific surface area of ZnCo2O4 decreases slightly with the introduction of CDs. The Raman spectra of bare ZnCo2O4 and CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 are shown in Fig. S5. The bands corresponding to the stretching vibrations of Co−O and Zn−O of ZnCo2O4 are observed [17]. No peaks of CDs are found in the range of 1300–1700 cm−1, which is owing to the low crystalline nature of CDs verified by HRTEM investigation (Fig. S6). The band shows a slight red shift and becomes wider, indicating the interaction between ZnCo2O4 and CDs.

Fig. 5 (a–c) shows the CV curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4 with various CDs loadings at different scan rates. A pair of redox peaks are distinct in a potential window from −0.05 to 0.65 V, which reveals that the pseudocapacitive behavior is mainly derived from Faradaic redox reactions. And the redox reactions are ascribed to the reversible transition between Co2+ and Co3+ that occurs in the potassium hydroxide solution prior to the oxygen evolution [14]. For all samples, the capacitive current density increases with the raise of the scan rate from 1 to 40 mV s−1, suggesting the remarkable electrochemical reactions. After introducing CDs into ZnCo2O4, the current peak intensity of the CV curves for CDs/ZnCo2O4 at every scan rate is increased and the shape of the corresponding curves does not change except for the shift of the peak positions with the increase of scan rates from 1 to 40 mV s−1. This means that the polarization of CDs/ZnCo2O4 electrode is relatively weak during the redox reactions, indicating the improved mass transportation and rapid electron transfer [23,36]. In particular, as shown in Fig. 5d, the polarization of CDs/ZnCo2O4 electrode can be alleviated with increasing the CDs loading content. And the CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 exhibits the highest peak intensity and the largest integral area in comparison with those of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0 and CDs/ZnCo2O4-1 at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. After the introduction of CD, the CV integration area becomes large. It is concluded that the specific capacitance of CDs/ZnCo2O4 is greatly enhanced due to the enhanced electrical conductivity. However, it is observed that the CDs/ZnCo2O4-1 delivers the decreased integral area in contrast to CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. This indicates that the specific capacitance starts to decrease with increasing CDs content. It is well known that CDs deliver the electric double-layer capacitance. Excess CDs occupy more surface of ZnCo2O4, which reduces the electroactive sites for faradaic reactions. This would result in the decrease of the specific capacitance of CDs/ZnCo2O4.

GCD curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4 electrodes at different current densities are shown in Fig. 6 (a–c). All the electrodes exhibit similar profile of asymmetric inverted triangles in a potential window ranging from 0 to 0.4 V. The plateaus in the discharge curves indicates the pseudocapacitive characteristics originated from redox reactions [37]. This phenomenon is consistent with the CV results. It is found that the CDs/ZnCo2O4 exhibits longer discharge time than those of ZnCo2O4 at various current densities. Especially, the discharge time of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 at 0.5 A/g reaches 1611 s which is 1.6 times high the value of bare ZnCo2O4. Based on GCD results, the specific capacitances are calculated according to the following eq. (1) [38].
(1)
C s = I Δt/m ΔV
where C s, I, Δt, m, and ΔV denote the specific capacitance (F/g), the discharge current (A), the discharge time (s), the mass of the electroactive material (g), and the potential window (V), respectively.

Fig. 6d demonstrates the correlation between the specific capacitance and the current density. The raise of current density leads to the decrease of specific capacitance gradually. This phenomenon is explained by the fact that the electrochemical processes occurred in the hierarchical structure cannot completely sustain high current density, considering the limitation of mass transport and electron transfer [34]. It can be seen that CDs/ZnCo2O4 presents the improved specific capacitance in comparison to those of bare ZnCo2O4 at various current densities. Especially, the optimal electrode of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 exhibits the highest capacity at a certain current density and also achieves a 78.5% of capacitance retention rate with increasing the current density from 0.5 to 8 A/g. The GCD of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 electrode is investigated at high density of 20, 40, and 80 A g−1 (Figure S7). The electrode exhibits the specific capacitance of 575, 420, and 220 F/g at 20, 40, and 80 A/g, respectively. The remarkable rate performance is originated from the enhanced electronic conductivity owing to the assistance of CDs and the reduced contact resistance due to the binder-free electrode [33]. Such feature can promote the electron transfer among different electroactive sites for the redox reactions. In addition, it should be point out that appropriate carbon components as conductive dopants are benefit to enhance the overall electrochemical performance of ZnCo2O4 electrode. Excess decoration of CDs endows relatively low capacity because of electrical double layer charge behavior. In addition, more CDs also reduce the electroactive sites of hierarchical ZnCo2O4, which would weaken the faradaic reactions. Table 1 summarizes the specific capacitance of ZnCo2O4-based electrodes for supercapacitors. The values of specific capacitance for CDs/ZnCo2O4 at 1 or 0.5 A/g in this work are higher than the reported values in previous studies.

The effect of CDs on the improved electrochemical performance of CDs/ZnCo2O4 is investigated by EIS. Fig. 6e shows the Nyquist plots of ZnCo2O4, CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 and CDs/ZnCo2O4-1.0 electrodes. The measured impedance spectroscopy is fitted by the equivalent circuit in the inset of Fig. 6e. Three electrodes illustrate similar intercepts of the quasi-semicircle in X-axis at high frequency region, representing small equivalent serial resistance (R ES) [17]. Such low junction resistance of 0.8 Ω is indicative of close contact ascribing to the direct growth of ZnCo2O4 film on nickel foam without binder-additive. CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 displays smallest semicircle diameter than those of bare ZnCo2O4 and CDs/ZnCo2O4-1.0. The charge transfer resistances of CDs/ZnCo2O4-1.0, CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 and ZnCo2O4 are determined to be 0.68, 0.7 and 0.9 Ω, respectively. Obviously, the suitable introduction of CDs into ZnCo2O4 can decrease the charge transfer resistance, achieving the rapid electron transfer at the interfaces [9]. This is in accordance with the CV and GCD results. Meanwhile, the straight line is indicative of Warburg impedance resistance (Z W) related to mass diffusion efficiency [34]. CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 displays a more vertical line in comparison to ZnCo2O4 at low frequency region, indicating the easier mass transport. The low mass diffusion resistance is probably attributed to hierarchical characteristic. Additionally, after decorating CDs into ZnCo2O4, the voids and open spaces may also be generated, which effectively facilitates the rapid diffusion of electrolyte ions.

The cyclic stability of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 was studied for consecutive 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 15 A/g. As shown in Fig. 6f, after initial 1000 cycles, the specific capacitance of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 reaches 105% of the initial value. A common activation process of the electrodes occurs with regard to the increased contact area between the electrode and electrolyte. Similar phenomenon has also been verified in the previous literatures [30]. Afterwards, the specific capacitance begins to decrease gradually and around 101% of the initial capacitance is maintained after 2000 cycles. With continuously increasing charge-discharge times up to 5000, the specific capacitance of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 retains 90% of its initial value. The capacitance loss is weak, which is probably attributed to the slight structural degradation induced by ion insertion and de-insertion during the charge–discharge processes [30]. In addition, 10% decay of the capacitance may be caused by the reduced interaction between electroactive CDs/ZnCo2O4 and Ni foam after continuous 5000 charge-discharge cycles. However, in view of nanowires-assembled hierarchical arrays supported on Ni foam, the structural degradation can be effectively alleviated, which ensures the CDs/ZnCo2O4 with long cycling life and weak capacitance fading. Such superior cyclic stability endows its practical applications as supercapacitor electrodes. Fig. 6f shows the Coulombic efficiency of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5 investigated over 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 15 A/g in 6 M KOH. The Coulombic efficiency of near 99.8% is achieved, demonstrating excellent cycling stability in electrochemical capacitor application.

An asymmetrical two-electrode system was fabricated to verify the practical application of the CDs/ZnCo2O4. Firstly, the mass ratio of CDs/ZnCo2O4 to AC is 0.22 according to the charge balance denoted as the following eq. (2) [32]:
(2)
m +/m − = C s − × ΔV −/C s + × ΔV +
where m, C
s , and ΔV are electroactive material mass, specific capacitance, and potential window, respectively. Secondly, the potential window of device is determined based on the CV curves of CDs/ZnCo2O4 and AC electrode. As shown in Fig. 7 a, different voltage windows of −0.05–0.65 V for CDs/ZnCo2O4 and -1–0 V for AC are confirmed at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. Thus, the scalable potential window of two-electrode system reaches 1.7 V. Fig. 7b shows the CV curves of the CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC system under the potential window in the range from 0 to 1.7 V. A quasi-rectangular profile indicates that the capacitance is derived from the combined contribution of faradaic and electrical double-layer capacitive behaviors [36]. Moreover, no remarkable deviation of CV curves is observed even at high scan rate of 50 mV s−1, suggesting the rapid current response upon voltage variation due to the fast charge–discharge characteristic [33]. The GCD curves of the two-electrode system with different current densities are illustrated in Fig. 7c. The nearly symmetric curves reveal the excellent electrochemical reversibility. The specific capacitance (C m), energy density (E), and power density (P) are calculated using following eqs. (3)–(5), respectively [25].
(3)
C m = I Δt/M ΔV
(4)
E = (1/7.2) C m (ΔV)2
(5)
P = E/Δt
where C m is the specific capacitance of the two-electrode system (F/g), M represents the total mass of the electroactive materials of two electrodes (g), ΔV is the potential drop during discharge (V), Δt refers to the discharging time (h), E is the energy density (W h/kg), and P denotes the power density (W/kg). As shown in Fig. 7d, the calculated capacitance is 136.8 F/g at 0.5 A/g. The capacitance retention still retains 24%, while the current density increases from 0.5 to 8 A/g. Fig. 7e displays the Ragone plot of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC device. The device delivers an energy density of 54.94 W h/kg at a power density of 425.3 W/kg, which is higher than that of other ZnCo2O4-based supercapacitors reported previously [9,43–46]. At a power density of 6804 W/kg, the energy density of 13.23 W h/kg can still be retained. Fig. 7f shows the cycling performance together with coulombic efficiency of CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC two-electrode system, which was performed by repeating galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles between 0 and 1.7 V at 10 A g−1. After continuous 5000 cycles, 92.5% of capacitance retention is kept and the coulombic efficiency reaches 94.9%. In addition, the digital image of the two-electrode system is illustrated in the inset of Fig. 7f. A red-light emitting diode can be powered, further demonstrating its practical application as a supercapacitor. The remarkable performances of the CDs/ZnCo2O4-0.5//AC supercapacitor are mainly originated from the following factors. Firstly, binder-free electrodes supported on current collectors exhibit low interfacial contact resistance [47]. Secondly, nanowires-assembled hierarchical structure increases the electrode/electrolyte contact areas and provides enough electroactive sites during faradaic reactions, achieving the larger specific capacitance [5]. Thirdly, the introduction of CDs conductive dopants improves the electronic conductivity, which is in favor of the fast transfer of electrons, thus enhancing the rate capability [20]. Moreover, the hierarchical porous structure can not only accommodate the electrolyte ions, but also buffer against the volume variation and alleviate the pulverization of electrodes, endowing the good cyclic stability [48]. All these combined effects can boost the overall electrochemical performance of CDs/ZnCo2O4. This study provides a simple and low-cost route for designing high-performance supercapacitor devices.

Nanowires-assembled hierarchical ZnCo2O4 arrays decorated with carbon dots were grown on Ni foam as binder-free electrodes by a facile hydrothermal method. Such hierarchical structure not only provides highly exposed electroactive sites and good electronic conductivity, but also facilitates the easy mass diffusion. The optimal CDs/ZnCo2O4 electrode can reach a specific capacitance of 1937 F/g at 1 A/g much higher than the 1192 F/g of bare ZnCo2O4. And 78.5% capacitance retention is achieved with the increase of current density from 0.5 to 8 A/g, indicating the excellent rate capability. After 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 15 A/g, 90% of the initial capacitance is retained which indicates the remarkable cycling lifetime. Two-electrode system assembled with CDs/ZnCo2O4 and activated carbon delivers a high energy density of 54.94 W h/kg at a power density of 425.3 W/kg and retains the energy density of 13.23 W h/kg at a high-power density of 6804 W/kg. These improved electrochemical performances are mainly associated with the factors including the exposed electroactive sites of hierarchical structure, high electrical conductivity accounting for CDs, and enhanced ion diffusion of electrolyte. This study provides a facile and low-cost avenue to design the transition metal oxide-based electrode materials for high-performance supercapacitors.

Caixia Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhe Zhao: Resources, Investigation, Methodology, Revision. Yang Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Qingshan Lu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under 51962026 and Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation under 2018MS05014.

ce:section.ce:para:
